insights: Customer Support has the fastest expense processing times (median 42 days) primarily because 69% of its expenses fall in the high-amount range (80k+), where organization-wide median processing time is 12–21 days faster than lower-amount bins.

The high-amount processing advantage is not universally transferable: four departments (HR, Development, IT, Product Management) either have zero high-amount expenses or show slower processing for them, making amount-based prioritization inapplicable or counterproductive in those units.

Recurring expenses process faster organization-wide (median 46.5 days) than One-time (58.5 days), directly contradicting Customer Support’s portfolio composition (68% One-time) and indicating its speed is not driven by a superior handling method for Recurring expenses.

Category-level speed patterns are inconsistent across departments: Services and Travel process quickly in Customer Support (medians 27.5 and 28.5 days) but are among the slowest in HR and Development (medians 62 and 68 days), ruling out category-specific process excellence as a scalable lever.

Customer Support’s exceptional North America performance (12-day median for high-amount expenses) reflects localized operational conditions rather than generalizable practice, as North America’s overall median is 36.5 days — meaning Customer Support’s result is an outlier within a fast region, not evidence of replicable workflow design.

Sales department achieves the largest high-amount processing advantage (-23.5 days) despite slower overall median time, confirming that high-amount concentration—not department identity—drives speed, and that Customer Support’s performance is portfolio-driven rather than practice-driven.
summary: The investigation reveals that Customer Support’s faster expense processing is fundamentally attributable to its atypical expense portfolio—dominated by high-amount, North America–based Services and Travel expenses—not to transferable process efficiencies. Organization-wide, high-amount expenses process significantly faster, but this pattern only applies to three departments; four others lack such expenses entirely or experience slower processing for them. Recurring expenses, which drive speed elsewhere, are underrepresented in Customer Support, further undermining claims of process superiority. Category and location advantages also fail consistency tests across departments, confirming that no single operational practice explains the speed differential. Therefore, cross-departmental efficiency gains cannot be achieved by emulating Customer Support’s 'practices'—instead, targeted portfolio rebalancing, selective high-amount prioritization where feasible, and root-cause analysis of why certain departments cannot handle high-amount expenses should be the focus of next-step initiatives.
